mapping.a_element: thermalization / entropy-maximization that selects the equilibrium distribution (Rayleigh-Jeans state) of the classical oscillator system
mapping.b_element: entropic-inference (maximum-entropy) principle that selects the stochastic dynamics from information constraints

mapping.a_element: conservation constraints imposed on the maximization: total energy and probability norm
mapping.b_element: information constraints supplied to the inference: continuity/directionality (continuous channel) and arrival rate plus first two jump-size moments (jump channel)

mapping.a_element: Lagrange multipliers conjugate to the conserved quantities (temperature, chemical potential) that pin down the RJ spectrum
mapping.b_element: the constraint parameters/multipliers (drift-diffusion coefficients, jump rate and moments) that pin down the Merton jump-diffusion

mapping.a_element: party vote fractions / system energies — the variable whose distribution is inferred
mapping.b_element: log price — the dynamical variable selected by the symmetry that markets reward returns

mapping.a_element: resulting Rayleigh-Jeans distribution reproducing the Lorenz and Pareto vote curves
mapping.b_element: resulting Merton jump-diffusion process (Kolmogorov-Feller equation) reproducing the implied-volatility smile

brief_justification: Both rest on the same transferable engine: a distribution/dynamics is fixed by maximizing entropy subject to the domain's constraints, the Jaynes correspondence linking equilibrium statistical mechanics to Bayesian/entropic inference. Paper A obtains the Rayleigh-Jeans state from conservation of energy and norm ('constraint driven condensation known in statistical mechanics'), while Paper B 'derives these processes from information constraints' and stresses that 'what changes from one model to another is not the inference, but the information supplied to it' — the same maximum-entropy machinery with different constraints, even though A yields an equilibrium distribution and B a derived stochastic process.
